sets.passage: In coastal marshes, plants do more than provide food for insects and birds. Their roots trap fine sediment that would otherwise be carried away by tides. Over time, this trapped material raises the surface of the marsh. The process is slow, but it helps some wetlands keep pace with moderate sea-level rise. Researchers have found that marshes with diverse plant communities often build soil more effectively than marshes dominated by a single species. One explanation is that different roots occupy different depths, creating a thicker underground network. However, if tides become too deep or too frequent, young plants cannot establish themselves, and the marsh may begin to break apart despite earlier soil growth. This is why restoration projects often measure both plant survival and sediment accumulation rather than counting new plants alone.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How marsh plants help wetlands maintain their surface
Why insects prefer diverse plant communities
How tides remove all sediment from coastal areas
Why single plant species always build soil fastest
questions.answer: How marsh plants help wetlands maintain their surface

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what do plant roots do?
options: Trap fine sediment
Increase the speed of tides
Prevent all sea-level rise
Carry birds' food underground
questions.answer: Trap fine sediment

questions.prompt: Why might diverse marshes build soil more effectively?
options: Their roots form networks at different depths
They receive no tidal water
They contain fewer insects
Their plants grow only on high ground
questions.answer: Their roots form networks at different depths

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about very deep or frequent tides?
options: They can prevent new marsh plants from surviving
They always make marshes more diverse
They remove the need for sediment trapping
They cause roots to grow above the surface
questions.answer: They can prevent new marsh plants from surviving

sets.passage: Many ancient cities were not planned all at once. Instead, they expanded through small decisions made by families, merchants, and local officials. A market might begin near a river crossing because traders could meet travelers there. Houses then appeared around the market, followed by workshops, storage buildings, and roads. Archaeologists can sometimes identify this gradual growth by studying street patterns. A city designed by a central authority often has straight roads and regular blocks. A city that grew from repeated local choices may have curved streets, uneven building sizes, and neighborhoods arranged around older paths. These irregular patterns are not evidence of disorder; they can reveal how people adapted the city to trade, terrain, and social relationships. Such evidence helps researchers reconstruct everyday decision-making even when written records are rare or incomplete.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Some ancient cities developed gradually through local choices
All ancient cities were designed with regular blocks
Archaeologists cannot learn from street patterns
Markets usually appeared after roads were completed
questions.answer: Some ancient cities developed gradually through local choices

questions.prompt: Why might a market begin near a river crossing?
options: Traders could meet travelers there
Central officials required straight roads there
Houses were forbidden in that area
The river made storage buildings unnecessary
questions.answer: Traders could meet travelers there

questions.prompt: What do curved streets and uneven blocks suggest?
options: Gradual adaptation to local needs
A complete lack of human planning
A city built only for religious ceremonies
A city abandoned before people lived there
questions.answer: Gradual adaptation to local needs

questions.prompt: The author mentions straight roads mainly to
options: contrast centrally planned cities with gradually grown cities
argue that straight roads were impossible in ancient times
show why markets always failed near rivers
describe the only pattern archaeologists study
questions.answer: contrast centrally planned cities with gradually grown cities

sets.passage: A seed bank is a facility that stores seeds under carefully controlled conditions. Low temperature and low moisture slow the chemical reactions that cause seeds to age. By preserving seeds, researchers protect genetic diversity that may be useful in the future. For example, a crop variety that is rarely planted today might contain resistance to a disease that becomes common later. Seed banks also support ecological restoration. If a damaged grassland needs to be replanted, stored seeds from local species can help rebuild a plant community similar to the original one. Still, seed banks are not perfect. Some tropical seeds cannot survive drying, and stored samples must be tested and replaced before they lose the ability to germinate. Managers therefore treat storage as an active scientific process, not simply as putting seeds on a shelf.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: The purposes and limits of seed banks
The fastest way to grow tropical crops
The chemical reactions used to create new seeds
The reasons farmers avoid genetic diversity
questions.answer: The purposes and limits of seed banks

questions.prompt: Why are seeds kept in low temperature and low moisture?
options: To slow aging processes
To make them larger
To encourage immediate germination
To remove genetic variation
questions.answer: To slow aging processes

questions.prompt: How can a rarely planted crop variety be useful later?
options: It may resist a future disease
It always produces more food
It cannot be affected by climate
It eliminates the need for restoration
questions.answer: It may resist a future disease

questions.prompt: What limitation of seed banks is mentioned?
options: Some seeds cannot survive drying
All stored seeds germinate too quickly
Local species cannot be stored
Seed banks only preserve flowers
questions.answer: Some seeds cannot survive drying

sets.passage: When people learn a new motor skill, such as playing a short piano pattern, improvement often continues after practice ends. This delayed improvement is linked to memory consolidation, a process in which the brain stabilizes and reorganizes recently learned information. Sleep appears to support consolidation, but the effect depends on the task. Skills that require accurate sequences may benefit from sleep because the brain can strengthen the order of actions without new distractions. However, practice quality still matters. Repeating a movement carelessly may consolidate mistakes as well as correct actions. For this reason, teachers often recommend short, focused sessions rather than long sessions in which attention declines. The recommendation reflects a balance between repetition and accuracy, not a belief that practice alone is automatically beneficial.

questions.prompt: What is the main topic of the passage?
options: How motor skills can improve after practice through consolidation
Why long practice sessions always produce the best results
How piano teachers choose musical styles
Why sleep replaces the need for practice
questions.answer: How motor skills can improve after practice through consolidation

questions.prompt: What does memory consolidation do?
options: Stabilizes and reorganizes learned information
Prevents people from remembering sequences
Makes all practice errors disappear
Reduces the need for focused attention
questions.answer: Stabilizes and reorganizes learned information

questions.prompt: Why can careless repetition be harmful?
options: Mistakes may also be consolidated
It makes sleep unnecessary
It prevents any movement from being remembered
It always strengthens attention
questions.answer: Mistakes may also be consolidated

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about short, focused practice sessions?
options: They help learners avoid consolidating careless errors
They are useful only for professional musicians
They work because they eliminate the need for sleep
They are less effective than distracted practice
questions.answer: They help learners avoid consolidating careless errors

sets.passage: Urban trees can reduce summer heat, but their effects vary across a city. A single row of young trees may provide shade only during part of the day, while a mature canopy can cool streets for longer periods. The type of surface nearby also matters. Asphalt stores heat and releases it slowly, so a shaded asphalt street may still remain warmer than a shaded area with soil or grass. City planners therefore study both tree cover and ground materials when designing heat-reduction projects. In neighborhoods with limited space, planting fewer but larger-canopy species may sometimes be more effective than planting many small ornamental trees that produce little shade. The best design depends on how shade, pavement, and pedestrian movement interact across an entire block.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Factors that influence how trees cool urban areas
Why ornamental trees grow faster than canopy trees
How asphalt prevents all trees from growing
Why cities should remove soil from streets
questions.answer: Factors that influence how trees cool urban areas

questions.prompt: Why can asphalt streets remain warm?
options: Asphalt stores and slowly releases heat
Asphalt creates more shade than soil
Young trees cannot grow near asphalt
Grass releases more heat than asphalt
questions.answer: Asphalt stores and slowly releases heat

questions.prompt: What do city planners study besides tree cover?
options: Ground materials
Bird migration routes
The color of traffic signs
The price of ornamental flowers
questions.answer: Ground materials

questions.prompt: What is suggested for neighborhoods with limited space?
options: Use species with larger canopies
Plant only small ornamental trees
Avoid trees near all streets
Replace all soil with asphalt
questions.answer: Use species with larger canopies

sets.passage: Glass beads found at archaeological sites can reveal long-distance trade connections. Because different workshops used distinct mixtures of minerals, scientists can analyze the chemical composition of a bead and compare it with known production centers. If a bead found far inland matches glass made near a coast, it may indicate that goods traveled through exchange networks. Researchers must be cautious, however. A bead could have been traded many times, carried as a personal object, or reused long after it was made. Chemical evidence therefore identifies possible origins, but it does not by itself explain every step of the bead's journey. Archaeologists compare chemical results with burial context, settlement patterns, and other artifacts before making broader claims. This combined approach reduces the risk of turning one object into a complete but unsupported trade story.

questions.prompt: What can chemical analysis of glass beads help identify?
options: Possible production origins
The exact person who owned each bead
The complete route of every trade journey
The age of all nearby buildings
questions.answer: Possible production origins

questions.prompt: Why do workshops matter in the analysis?
options: They used distinct mineral mixtures
They always wrote labels on beads
They were all located far inland
They never traded their products
questions.answer: They used distinct mineral mixtures

questions.prompt: What does the author warn about?
options: Chemical evidence cannot show every step of an object's movement
Glass beads cannot survive at archaeological sites
Coastal workshops never made glass
Trade networks avoided personal objects
questions.answer: Chemical evidence cannot show every step of an object's movement

questions.prompt: The phrase "by itself" emphasizes that chemical evidence
options: needs to be combined with other information
is always incorrect
can identify modern owners
removes the need for caution
questions.answer: needs to be combined with other information

sets.passage: In some lakes, tiny organisms called plankton move up and down in the water each day. During the night, many rise toward the surface to feed on algae. During the day, they sink to deeper water, where they are less visible to fish. This pattern is known as vertical migration. It may seem inefficient because swimming uses energy, but the behavior can increase survival. Light levels, water temperature, and the presence of predators all influence how far the plankton move. When fish are absent, the organisms may remain closer to the surface even during daylight. The pattern shows how a simple daily movement can connect feeding opportunities with the risk of being eaten. Scientists can test this relationship by comparing lakes with different fish populations and light conditions.

questions.prompt: What is vertical migration?
options: Daily movement up and down in the water
A permanent move from one lake to another
The process by which algae become fish
A change in water temperature caused by sunlight
questions.answer: Daily movement up and down in the water

questions.prompt: Why do many plankton sink during the day?
options: To become less visible to fish
To find more sunlight
To avoid eating algae
To leave the lake permanently
questions.answer: To become less visible to fish

questions.prompt: What factor is mentioned as affecting how far plankton move?
options: The presence of predators
The size of nearby trees
The shape of the shoreline
The age of the lake's rocks
questions.answer: The presence of predators

questions.prompt: What can be inferred if fish are not present?
options: Plankton may stay nearer the surface in daylight
Plankton will stop feeding at night
Algae will disappear from the lake
Water temperature will no longer matter
questions.answer: Plankton may stay nearer the surface in daylight

sets.passage: The invention of movable type did not immediately make books cheap everywhere. Printing required skilled workers, presses, paper, ink, and a market large enough to cover costs. In regions where paper was expensive or literacy was limited, handwritten manuscripts continued to circulate alongside printed books. Printers also had to choose texts carefully. A religious text or schoolbook with steady demand was safer to print than a new poem that might attract only a few readers. Thus, printing technology changed communication gradually, shaped by materials, labor, and the needs of local communities. The technology was powerful, but its impact depended on the institutions and readers surrounding it. In this sense, printing was both a technical invention and a practical business decision.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Printing spread gradually because economic and social conditions mattered
Movable type instantly made all books inexpensive
Handwritten manuscripts disappeared everywhere at once
Printers preferred new poems over schoolbooks
questions.answer: Printing spread gradually because economic and social conditions mattered

questions.prompt: Why might manuscripts continue to circulate?
options: Paper was expensive or literacy was limited
Printing presses required no workers
Printed books were illegal everywhere
Religious texts could not be printed
questions.answer: Paper was expensive or literacy was limited

questions.prompt: Why was a schoolbook safer to print than a new poem?
options: It had steadier demand
It required no paper
It was always shorter
It could only be copied by hand
questions.answer: It had steadier demand

questions.prompt: The passage uses the phrase "alongside printed books" to show that manuscripts
options: coexisted with printed materials
were physically attached to books
were never read by students
were produced by printing presses
questions.answer: coexisted with printed materials

sets.explain_zh: Academic reading explanation: the correct answers are directly supported by the passage and the distractors contradict or overstate the text.

sets.passage: Some desert animals avoid extreme heat not by tolerating it directly but by changing when they are active. A small mammal may spend the hottest hours in a burrow and search for food at night. This strategy reduces water loss because cooler air slows evaporation from the body. Yet night activity has trade-offs. Food may be harder to locate in darkness, and nocturnal predators may be more active. Biologists study these trade-offs to understand how animals respond to warming climates. If nights become warmer, species that depend on nighttime cooling may need to shift their behavior again. Their responses may differ by species, since diet, body size, and shelter availability all affect the costs of being active. For this reason, researchers avoid assuming that all desert species will react in the same way.

questions.prompt: What strategy is described in the passage?
options: Changing activity times to avoid heat
Drinking seawater during the day
Growing thicker fur in summer
Living only in open sunlight
questions.answer: Changing activity times to avoid heat

questions.prompt: How does night activity reduce water loss?
options: Cooler air slows evaporation
Darkness creates more rain
Burrows produce fresh water
Food contains no moisture at night
questions.answer: Cooler air slows evaporation

questions.prompt: What trade-off of night activity is mentioned?
options: Nocturnal predators may be more active
All food becomes easier to find
Animals cannot enter burrows
The body loses heat too quickly
questions.answer: Nocturnal predators may be more active

questions.prompt: What might happen if nights become warmer?
options: Some species may need to adjust their behavior again
All desert animals will become active only at noon
Evaporation will stop completely
Predators will disappear from deserts
questions.answer: Some species may need to adjust their behavior again

sets.explain_zh: Academic reading explanation: the correct answers are directly supported by the passage and the distractors contradict or overstate the text.

sets.passage: Maps are often treated as neutral pictures of space, but every map involves choices. A mapmaker decides what area to show, which features to label, and how to represent distance. A subway map, for example, may distort actual geography so that station connections are easier to understand. This distortion is useful for riders, even though it would be misleading for measuring walking distance. Historical maps require similar caution. A map made for sailors may emphasize coastlines and harbors while ignoring inland roads. To interpret a map well, readers must ask what problem the map was designed to solve. This question helps explain why two accurate maps of the same place can look very different. The same landscape may be simplified differently for navigation, teaching, or political administration.

questions.prompt: What is the main point of the passage?
options: Maps reflect choices made for particular purposes
All maps show distance with perfect accuracy
Subway maps are useful for measuring walking routes
Historical maps ignored coastlines
questions.answer: Maps reflect choices made for particular purposes

questions.prompt: Why might a subway map distort geography?
options: To make station connections clearer
To hide the names of stations
To help sailors find harbors
To measure exact walking distance
questions.answer: To make station connections clearer

questions.prompt: What would a map for sailors likely emphasize?
options: Coastlines and harbors
Inland classroom locations
Subway transfer times
The size of library shelves
questions.answer: Coastlines and harbors

questions.prompt: What does the author suggest readers should consider?
options: The purpose the map was designed to serve
Whether all maps should be avoided
How to remove labels from maps
Why geography never changes
questions.answer: The purpose the map was designed to serve

sets.explain_zh: Academic reading explanation: the correct answers are directly supported by the passage and the distractors contradict or overstate the text.
